season where I am totally off the chain in terms of my normal diet even then I'll do intermittent fasting and I do that one for cognition it is hugely valuable to staying sharp to have these long periods of time without eating and then usually one to two times a year I'll do an extended fast and some of them might only be 24 hours other times it might be up to 72 hours I've done a full five day fast before that was miserable though and I don't plan to be repeating that unless some new evidence comes out that really shows that there's a huge difference between the 72-hour fast and a five-day fast I just found that it was like having the flu I couldn't pay attention it was just horrible and I'm somebody who's keto adapted so for me to really struggle at the five days like it it negatively impacted my business which is something that I don't like to do and certainly had a negative impact on my cognition I find a 24-hour fast it's pretty amazing longer than that and I do find that it has negative impact on my performance so I do limit that but every day literally every day my average is probably about 17.5 hours so that means there are some days that I'm doing call it 19 hours and then other days in the weekend uh we're all due typically 16 ish hours but in the wash it comes out to about 17.5 when you factor it over the course of a year but that intermittent fasting is something that I do all the time every day it's just a natural part of my life and the way that I do that is I have my last meal around 1 15 2 p.m at the latest and then I don't eat again until the following morning and then I'll usually eat somewhere between eight and nine sometimes a little earlier sometimes a little bit later depending on what I'm doing that day but making sure that I get that average of 17.5 hours has been incredibly beneficial so there's all kinds of health benefits but in terms of its impact on body composition and cognition it really can't be beat especially when your diet consists of whole food and that's the thing other than the olive oil everything that you see here on this table is the thing that you would see out and about so the bacon looks like the Flesh of a pig as it is and the eggs obviously are just whole eggs the carrots carrots the berries berries the vegetable vegetables and so not eating processed food eating Whole Food where during the week I'm going to guess 95 of my calories even including a week where I cheat 92 to 95 of my calories are going to come from Whole Food and that's that's been a huge win in terms of how I feel how I sleep cognition all of it is vastly improved by whole food so that's my easy advice to people is to eat whole food whenever possible and that's my diet all right let's talk about the Big Daddy of all things related to cognitive optimization and that is sleep if I were going to say the two most important things that you can do to optimize your cognition is sleep and eat right those are the big ones but sleep is the one that will impact you the fastest you could mess up your diet for a day two days maybe even a week before it really starts to hammer you but man you miss one night's sleep and there is a catastrophic Cascade of biological problems that will present themselves rapidly so somebody that gets I think less than six hours of sleep a night shows the same kind of blood glucose response as somebody who's pre-diabetic so that gives you an idea of missing only part of one night's sleep can have that kind of massive cellular impact so being incredibly thoughtful about getting your sleep is super important now as somebody who loves hustle porn and being hardcore and more working an amazing amount I do all of that with precious little negative impact to my sleep I won't say that it never happens especially not since I've gotten into nfts it is there has been some wobble in my perfect sleep record but it's really minor and the reason that I do that is to me being tired is a unique ring of hell I do not understand people who are prepared to go their entire lives fatigued it makes everything worse your ability to enjoy your life goes down you're likely to being depressed goes way up your likelihood of having metabolic disturbances goes way up there there's just so many things go wrong and then of course the most traumatically impacted is your ability to think clearly and think quickly if you've ever gone even one night with bad sleep let alone two or three nights you can feel yourself declining rapidly in performance in fact if you take somebody that hasn't gotten any sleep and put them in a car they have the same sort of delays that somebody who's intoxicated has so the impacts on cognition cannot be overstated so be very careful if you're trying to be at your best and really perform at an elite level the thing that you must do is get sleep so make sure that you're prioritizing your sleep now how do we do that how do we get sleep done in a high quality way number one is you want to make sure that you're sleeping in a completely dark environment so no Night Lights hopefully no even like charging lights or things like that now one way I do to make myself absolutely bulletproof not only do I have blackout curtains but I sleep with the blankets completely over my head now admittedly it didn't start for reasons of light pollution but now as I get older I'm actually grateful that my whole life I've had this pattern of sleeping with the blankets up over my head which I know for some people is a just nightmare scenario but for me it's like I mean if I'm honest it's like sleeping in a womb it's just to be completely encased is so wonderful and it makes me feel so relaxed absolutely incredible uh and then the other thing that I do is I use white noise and so to make sure that there are no little noises that are going to wake me up in the middle of the night I have white noise basically sounds like a fan um but from a little machine that plays that consistent sound and that helps me sleep incredibly well and then because my mind is so frequently in problem solving mode what I've found is that about 30 minutes before I go to bed I switch into narrative mode and so I'm gonna be reading a book I use Audible but I'm going to be listening to a book that shifts my mind out of problem solving mode into narrative mode and then if I wake up in the middle of the night I keep next to me three pairs of iPod Pros so that I can put them in I listen to the book it makes sure that I don't go into problem solving mode that I can can stay there in narrative mode and I turn the volume down just to the point where a little bit of pressure on my ear is required to hear them perfectly and so I'll put that bit of pressure listen to the story and then as I fall asleep that pressure comes off my ear and now I can barely hear it and I fall right back to sleep now I will give you the advanced tip which is don't pick an audiobook that has a bunch of murder in it because then you wake up to the narrator screaming and yelling and that was waking me up so I had to learn the hard way that there's a certain type of book that's a good engaging story but doesn't have narration where you know there's yelling and screaming at any point in the story and yeah the last thing that I will say about my sleep is that I go to bed at the same time every night which is 9 p.m I treat it like a religion I go to bed every night at that time sometimes a few minutes earlier if I can convince Lisa and then the second part of that is that I don't set an alarm so I haven't set an alarm for the last 16 17 years at this point so trust me you can do amazing incredible things in your life without needing to set an alarm I get as much sleep as I need now I don't have kids so that is no doubt a big part of how I'm able to maintain that but the more that if you do have kids you can sleep closer to their sleep rhythms to make sure that you're getting as much sleep as you need and let me tell you if I had kids and I needed to go to bed at 7 pm I would go to bed at 7 pm so I'm gonna go to bed at whatever time I need to in order to get all the sleep that I need if you need nine hours get nine hours your brain shrinks in the middle of the night which basically the inflammation is going down so that the glial system can flush everything out get rid of any toxins that might be building up in the system get rid of the amyloid plaques which are very present in people that have Alzheimer's probably doesn't cause Alzheimer's but nonetheless lets you know that it's doing some something that we don't necessarily want sticking around and I said the glial system and I think I mean the lymphatic system somebody will have to fact check me on that because I'm actually not sure which but the brain shrinks nonetheless and allows you to clear out that system so being very thoughtful to get as much sleep as you need so that you can enjoy your life so that on a biological cellular level that you're able to run all your processes so that you're able to do all the memory consolidation all the things that go along with the ancillary effects to sleeping and then making sure that you're able to think as clearly sharply and quickly as possible and all of those things come down to the quality of your sleep the quality of your diet the quality of your exercise and your ability to manage your psychological life which we can do most easily through meditation so that in a bundle my friends is how you take care of yourself cognitively to make sure that you can deliver an elite level of performance day after day week after week month after month year after year if you need more help putting together an action plan on how to improve yourself and your life check out this episode where I detail one of the first major steps stopping procrastination and kicking laziness out all together if you're not going in the right direction in your life ![](https://tombilyeu.com/hubfs/Atlas%20Template/Tom%20(3).png)

Hey, I’m Tom.I help average people live extraordinary lives.

![](https://tombilyeu.com/hubfs/Atlas%20Template/tom-portrait-mobile.png)

# Let's get one thing straight. Your potential is limitless.

If you’re feeling stuck. Overwhelmed. Or frustrated with where you are in life I've been there.

I was the guy who couldn't get out of bed. **My mother thought I'd flunk out of college**. Then I decided to take control of my life and everything changed.

Fast forward: I co-founded Quest Nutrition. **Sold it for a billion dollars.** Now, I lead Impact Theory, a media company whose original content has earned over a billion views.

My success didn’t come from being special. It came from adopting a few simple mindset shifts. Shifts I will teach you.

The world is desperate for leaders, entrepreneurs, and game-changers. **Is that you?**

This journey to extraordinary isn’t easy. But it's 100% worth it.

I’ll show you how to:

- Believe in yourself

- Build discipline

- Create practical plans to achieve your goals


This is about so much more than money. **It's about becoming who you're meant to be.**

Ready to transform your life? Let's get to work. Your journey to legendary starts now.

# What’s Your Next Step?

Choose the path that best describes

where you are right now:

Attention Entrepreneurs

## Launching a Business

You’ve got the idea, now it’s time to build. Get the blueprint to validate your idea, launch on a budget, and build momentum fast. You’ll master productivity, gain clarity, and finally turn your vision into income.


[Explore Zero To Founder](https://tombilyeu.com/zero-to-founder)

## Building Ironclad Habits

You don’t need more motivation. You need clarity, discipline, and a system that actually works. Learn how to rewire your mind, stop procrastinating, and build unshakable habits so you can take control of your life and crush your goals.


[DiscoverBillion Dollar Habits](https://tombilyeu.com/billiondollarhabits)

I’ve helped 18,000+ students achieve their goals.

Before I encountered Impact Theory I was running a business that gave me no joy, in an industry that delivers a negative societal outcome. I’m not driven by money for money’s sake, so I was in this state of limbo, and every adversity that came my way felt like the final nail in the coffin for the business, and my mental and emotional health too.

Since dedicating myself to learning about what Tom has to say, I have not only been able to get myself back in the drivers seat of my business, but I now have the confidence and gusto to take on a challenge I never considered possible- steering my industry in a better direction.

I now have a belief system that picks me up when the world is crashing down around me, and I have far more actionable tactics at my disposal to sharpen my business’ output. I have a much clearer view of what the landscape of business, culture, and society in general actually looks like, which I consider to be a super power.

I’ve always had high self esteem, but the life I had built for myself was sucking my mental health like a leech. Now I realise what I’m capable of, my goals keep getting bigger and all the data I have tells me that I’m entirely unstoppable.

Since dedicating myself to learning about what Tom has to say, I have not only been able to get myself back in the drivers seat of my business, but I now have the confidence and gusto to take on a challenge I never considered possible- steering my industry in a better direction.

I now have a belief system that picks me up when the world is crashing down around me, and I have far more actionable tactics at my disposal to sharpen my business’ output. I have a much clearer view of what the landscape of business, culture, and society in general actually looks like, which I consider to be a super power.

I’ve always had high self esteem, but the life I had built for myself was sucking my mental health like a leech. Now I realise what I’m capable of, my goals keep getting bigger and all the data I have tells me that I’m entirely unstoppable.

Tiffany Scarpelli

I’m not sure how I stumbled upon Tom Bilyeu’s YouTube interviews but once I found the first one, I became a bit of an addict around 2022/2023, watching them once or twice per week from around 7pm until way past my bedtime. (This is before joining ITU and learning about rules (like bedtime and wakeup rules).

I’m a wellness enthusiast and I initially was drawn to the many interviews Tom would have with some of the top medical experts on disease prevention and like topics. I’d hear Tom say that he is not that smart, yet as I examined his ability to process so much information about the person he was interviewing including reading their book before the interview, resulting in asking great questions and getting the interviewee to explain their knowledge in depth, making the interview best in class, I wanted to learn more from Tom and I wanted to learn how to process information like a speed demon as well so I joined Impact Theory University.

In addition to being a lover of wellness, I am a person who always strives to improve. I grew up with a stigma that I was not that smart, due to being diagnosed at an early age with a “learning disability”. I’ve allowed this “self limiting belief” and “identity” (taught in ITU) to control me far into adulthood no matter how much or how often I’ve succeeded at something.

While I had learned at an early age after 5 years of improvisation classes and community theater (starting at age 9, urged by my mom to help me break out of extreme shyness) that if I work harder and longer and do the thing I’m scared of, I can overcome a fear and achieve a desired goal, most of my life I’d stop or quit when I hit one of life’s many roadblocks. I didn’t understand “the only belief that matters” concept that Tom teaches in ITU.

I had a habit of blaming the external thing/s and myself for, in my eyes, being less smart or less capable than others (Identity concept taught in ITU). I’d chalk it up to being a slow learner and “that’s just how I am”, among other excuses. Tom also teaches about neuroplasticity in ITU. I now understand that I can learn to learn faster and more deeply and significantly improve my memory because my brain can physically change when I decide to master a desired skill/subject because the brain forms and strengthens connections between neurons, meaning my brain literally has the ability to become smarter, which has opened my eyes to an entirely new playing field. Thank you Tom and ITU team!

I am continuously working on my growth mindset (taught in ITU) in this area and others. I’ve learned that it’s okay to fail and that’s actually part of the learning process. So, now, it’s not a huge shock when I hit a roadblock and I understand that it doesn’t mean it’s time to quit. If you don’t believe an old dog can learn new tricks, you haven’t met me!

I am currently running a successful solo wellness business, happily learning and reading much faster and my memory is top notch (due to what I’ve learned in ITU and from other self development gurus, new “brain” supplements and eating better quality foods thanks to learning from a few of Tom’s YT interviews). Lastly, I am currently reading Lisa Bilyeu’s book, “Radical Confidence” and I’m absolutely loving her stories and lessons about Quest Nutrition, growth and confidence. Thank you Tom, Lisa and ITU team for the amazing work you do. It’s appreciated!

Tiffany Scarpelli

I’m not sure how I stumbled upon Tom Bilyeu’s YouTube interviews but once I found the first one, I became a bit of an addict around 2022/2023, watching them once or twice per week from around 7pm until way past my bedtime. (This is before joining ITU and learning about rules (like bedtime and wakeup rules).

I’m a wellness enthusiast and I initially was drawn to the many interviews Tom would have with some of the top medical experts on disease prevention and like topics. I’d hear Tom say that he is not that smart, yet as I examined his ability to process so much information about the person he was interviewing including reading their book before the interview, resulting in asking great questions and getting the interviewee to explain their knowledge in depth, making the interview best in class, I wanted to learn more from Tom and I wanted to learn how to process information like a speed demon as well so I joined Impact Theory University.

In addition to being a lover of wellness, I am a person who always strives to improve. I grew up with a stigma that I was not that smart, due to being diagnosed at an early age with a “learning disability”. I’ve allowed this “self limiting belief” and “identity” (taught in ITU) to control me far into adulthood no matter how much or how often I’ve succeeded at something.

While I had learned at an early age after 5 years of improvisation classes and community theater (starting at age 9, urged by my mom to help me break out of extreme shyness) that if I work harder and longer and do the thing I’m scared of, I can overcome a fear and achieve a desired goal, most of my life I’d stop or quit when I hit one of life’s many roadblocks. I didn’t understand “the only belief that matters” concept that Tom teaches in ITU.

I had a habit of blaming the external thing/s and myself for, in my eyes, being less smart or less capable than others (Identity concept taught in ITU). I’d chalk it up to being a slow learner and “that’s just how I am”, among other excuses. Tom also teaches about neuroplasticity in ITU. I now understand that I can learn to learn faster and more deeply and significantly improve my memory because my brain can physically change when I decide to master a desired skill/subject because the brain forms and strengthens connections between neurons, meaning my brain literally has the ability to become smarter, which has opened my eyes to an entirely new playing field. Thank you Tom and ITU team!

I am continuously working on my growth mindset (taught in ITU) in this area and others. I’ve learned that it’s okay to fail and that’s actually part of the learning process. So, now, it’s not a huge shock when I hit a roadblock and I understand that it doesn’t mean it’s time to quit. If you don’t believe an old dog can learn new tricks, you haven’t met me!

I am currently running a successful solo wellness business, happily learning and reading much faster and my memory is top notch (due to what I’ve learned in ITU and from other self development gurus, new “brain” supplements and eating better quality foods thanks to learning from a few of Tom’s YT interviews). Lastly, I am currently reading Lisa Bilyeu’s book, “Radical Confidence” and I’m absolutely loving her stories and lessons about Quest Nutrition, growth and confidence. Thank you Tom, Lisa and ITU team for the amazing work you do. It’s appreciated!

H

Henri

At ITU, a lot of us are like-minded. We want to conquer the world but most of all we want to design our own life. While designing our own life, we stumble upon ourselves. Especially, when it comes to the fascinating mechanisms in our body, playing games in and with our mind. The body constantly changes the mindset in our head. The body wants to survive, our head wants to move on. In between stands resistance. These are the moments when we talk about postponing, too much analyzing, feeling afraid of doing, procrastination, holding back and resistance. This is where ITU and the 90-day challenges are very helpful. When talking out loud to others about the issues we are dealing with, other students have a different view on these issues which they can share. It brings a different or new perspective and that helps. Furthermore, at ITU we become accountable to others. We can share our goals but are kept accountable towards the others in taking the necessary actions to reach these goals. Meeting weekly with students from all over the world, sharing worldviews out of different cultures is an eyeopener. At ITU, one can learn a lot. And constant learning is a very important tool in designing our own life for the good. Belief in your dreams, act upon, learn by doing, reach your goals.

#WeekendReflections How Tom Bilyeu's Vision Transformed My Mindset 🌟

Have you ever had a moment that completely shifted your perspective on life and success? For me, that moment came when I discovered Tom Bilyeu and Impact Theory - [impacttheory.com](https://impacttheory.com/).

Why did Tom Bilyeu make such a profound impact on me?

Tom's story is one of relentless perseverance and unwavering belief in the power of the human mind. From co-founding Quest Nutrition to building Impact Theory, Tom’s journey is a masterclass in resilience and visionary thinking.

The Journey:
Early Struggles: Tom didn't start at the top. He faced numerous challenges and setbacks. Hearing his honest account of these struggles made me realize that success is not a straight line but a series of ups and downs.

Creating Quest Nutrition: Tom’s leap into the nutrition industry with Quest Nutrition is a testament to his entrepreneurial spirit. He didn’t just create a product; he revolutionized an industry by focusing on health and quality.

Founding Impact Theory: Driven by a mission to help people unlock their potential, Tom founded Impact Theory. Through this platform, he interviews the world’s top achievers, providing insights and wisdom that are both profound and actionable.

Mindset Mastery: One of Tom’s core teachings is the importance of mindset. His emphasis on growth, resilience, and positive thinking has helped me reshape my approach to challenges and opportunities.

Building a Community: Tom has cultivated a global community of learners and doers. Being part of this community has provided me with support, inspiration, and the motivation to keep pushing forward.

The Result:
Thanks to Tom Bilyeu, I've adopted a growth mindset that has transformed both my personal and professional life. His teachings have empowered me to take bold steps, embrace failures, and continuously strive for improvement.

Key Takeaway:
Tom Bilyeu’s story is a powerful reminder that with the right mindset and relentless determination, we can achieve extraordinary things. If you haven’t yet, dive into Impact Theory—you might just find the inspiration you need to take your life to the next level.

H

Henri

At ITU, a lot of us are like-minded. We want to conquer the world but most of all we want to design our own life. While designing our own life, we stumble upon ourselves. Especially, when it comes to the fascinating mechanisms in our body, playing games in and with our mind. The body constantly changes the mindset in our head. The body wants to survive, our head wants to move on. In between stands resistance. These are the moments when we talk about postponing, too much analyzing, feeling afraid of doing, procrastination, holding back and resistance. This is where ITU and the 90-day challenges are very helpful. When talking out loud to others about the issues we are dealing with, other students have a different view on these issues which they can share. It brings a different or new perspective and that helps. Furthermore, at ITU we become accountable to others. We can share our goals but are kept accountable towards the others in taking the necessary actions to reach these goals. Meeting weekly with students from all over the world, sharing worldviews out of different cultures is an eyeopener. At ITU, one can learn a lot. And constant learning is a very important tool in designing our own life for the good. Belief in your dreams, act upon, learn by doing, reach your goals.

#WeekendReflections How Tom Bilyeu's Vision Transformed My Mindset 🌟

Have you ever had a moment that completely shifted your perspective on life and success? For me, that moment came when I discovered Tom Bilyeu and Impact Theory - [impacttheory.com](https://impacttheory.com/).

Why did Tom Bilyeu make such a profound impact on me?

Tom's story is one of relentless perseverance and unwavering belief in the power of the human mind. From co-founding Quest Nutrition to building Impact Theory, Tom’s journey is a masterclass in resilience and visionary thinking.

The Journey:
Early Struggles: Tom didn't start at the top. He faced numerous challenges and setbacks. Hearing his honest account of these struggles made me realize that success is not a straight line but a series of ups and downs.

Creating Quest Nutrition: Tom’s leap into the nutrition industry with Quest Nutrition is a testament to his entrepreneurial spirit. He didn’t just create a product; he revolutionized an industry by focusing on health and quality.

Founding Impact Theory: Driven by a mission to help people unlock their potential, Tom founded Impact Theory. Through this platform, he interviews the world’s top achievers, providing insights and wisdom that are both profound and actionable.

Mindset Mastery: One of Tom’s core teachings is the importance of mindset. His emphasis on growth, resilience, and positive thinking has helped me reshape my approach to challenges and opportunities.

Building a Community: Tom has cultivated a global community of learners and doers. Being part of this community has provided me with support, inspiration, and the motivation to keep pushing forward.

The Result:
Thanks to Tom Bilyeu, I've adopted a growth mindset that has transformed both my personal and professional life. His teachings have empowered me to take bold steps, embrace failures, and continuously strive for improvement.

Key Takeaway:
Tom Bilyeu’s story is a powerful reminder that with the right mindset and relentless determination, we can achieve extraordinary things. If you haven’t yet, dive into Impact Theory—you might just find the inspiration you need to take your life to the next level.

How has a mentor or role model impacted your life? Share your story below!

Lisa Grace

What challenges were you facing before starting to learn from Tom?
Emotional self-regulation, feeling beaten up and let down by a lifetime corporate career, elusive search for like-minded people.

What progress have you made applying what you learned?
I’m not afraid of failure. I can harness my natural curiosity to continuously experiment and have fun doing it! I’m currently on the verge of a viable MVP for a coaching practice AI-enabled app, and throughout the idea development phase, have embraced the fact that I might be the only one in love with it. And if I find that’s the case, I’ll be disappointed, but move on to my next idea, taking along my insights along the way. Before Impact Theory, I think I would be crushed to work my ass off and fail.
How has that impacted your life and how you feel about yourself?
The ITU community support and courses have been 10x more valuable than my MBA (which took 20 years to pay off). I feel more confident in general, and definitely more confident and motivated to become the entrepreneur I’ve always wanted to be! Thank you Tom and ITU Team. <3

I am becoming more at peace with failing and using it as a tool rather than collapsing and feeling like a failure. I am also learning I am worthy and it’s exciting to know by learning this and believing it more and more I’ll be able to achieve the things I set out to achieve.

Digital Strategist who gets results and can give you a kick-ass workout

[![linkedin-post](https://embed-v2.testimonial.to/images/linkedin-embed-logo.png)](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7221956051565211648)

During a pivotal moment, I watched an AMA with Tom Bilyeu on Facebook (back when I used the platform). I asked how to navigate my anxieties about asking questions and my fear of looking stupid.

His response was a game-changer: always ask, even if it feels uncomfortable and you THINK you will look "stupid". Guess what? I rarely looked "stupid" for seeking clarity. The truth is, the power of knowledge far outweighs the fleeting embarrassment of not having all the answers.

Jul 24, 2024

![Lisa Grace](https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/testimonialto.appspot.com/o/testimonials%2F66880897-e7ed-4cde-b788-5550cd832b74%2Favatar?alt=media&token=cf28d405-a47b-4ab8-aa6d-c43600023d77)

Lisa Grace

What challenges were you facing before starting to learn from Tom?
Emotional self-regulation, feeling beaten up and let down by a lifetime corporate career, elusive search for like-minded people.

What progress have you made applying what you learned?
I’m not afraid of failure. I can harness my natural curiosity to continuously experiment and have fun doing it! I’m currently on the verge of a viable MVP for a coaching practice AI-enabled app, and throughout the idea development phase, have embraced the fact that I might be the only one in love with it. And if I find that’s the case, I’ll be disappointed, but move on to my next idea, taking along my insights along the way. Before Impact Theory, I think I would be crushed to work my ass off and fail.
How has that impacted your life and how you feel about yourself?
The ITU community support and courses have been 10x more valuable than my MBA (which took 20 years to pay off). I feel more confident in general, and definitely more confident and motivated to become the entrepreneur I’ve always wanted to be! Thank you Tom and ITU Team. <3
